science_meta_description: In-app encyclopedia of orbital mechanics, transfers, propulsion, mission phases, scales & time, and porkchop reading — every formula and named term Orrery references.
science_intro: Every term, formula, and named law the simulator references — explained where you'll meet them.
science_tab_empty: Sections for this tab are still being authored.
chip_label_v_infinity: V∞ — leftover speed at the destination
chip_label_orbit_insertion: Orbit insertion — the braking burn at the destination
chip_label_trans_x_injection: TLI/TMI — the big departure burn from Earth orbit
chip_label_met: MET — mission elapsed time, the universal stopwatch
chip_label_vis_viva: Vis-viva — speed from radius and orbit size
chip_label_light_minute: Light-minute — radio-signal lag at this distance
chip_label_semi_major_axis: Semi-major axis — the orbit's size; sets the period
chip_label_inclination: Inclination — tilt of the orbit plane
chip_label_apsides_perihelion: Perihelion — closest approach to the Sun
chip_label_apsides_aphelion: Aphelion — farthest point from the Sun
chip_label_departure_axis: Departure axis — the calendar of launch windows
chip_label_tof_axis: Time-of-flight axis — fast vs slow trips
chip_label_dv_heatmap: Colour scale — cheap teal to expensive red
chip_label_orbit_regimes: Orbit regimes — LEO / MEO / GEO / HEO altitude bands
chip_label_mission_types: Mission types — flyby, orbiter, lander, rover, sample return
plan_explainer_intro: Each cell asks: "if I launch on date X and arrive on date X+TOF, how much fuel does it take?" Lower numbers = cheaper missions.
plan_explainer_y_desc: How long the trip takes (in days).
plan_explainer_y_desc_years: How long the trip takes (in years).
plan_explainer_color_desc: Cool teal = cheap (~3 km/s). Yellow = moderate. Red = very expensive (>9 km/s).
plan_explainer_how_to_desc: Click a cool-colored region near the natural Hohmann minimum (~250-day TOF, every ~26 months) — that's the cheapest launch window. The right panel shows the exact dates and ∆v.
plan_load_failed: Failed to load porkchop grid for this destination.
plan_mission_type_disabled_gas_giant: Landing on a gas giant is not a defined manoeuvre. See Galileo's orbit insertion in the mission library.
plan_mission_type_disabled_flyby_only: Landing is not a defined manoeuvre for this destination at this fidelity. Use FLYBY or see the mission library for real flight paths.
plan_gravity_assist_caveat: Direct trajectory shown. Real outer-planet missions use planetary gravity assists — see Voyager 2, Galileo, Cassini, New Horizons in Missions for actual flight paths.
plan_empty_hint_desktop: Tap a cell on the porkchop plot. Cool blue zones are cheap; hot red zones are expensive launch energy.
plan_empty_hint_mobile: On mobile, touch and hold the plot to open the magnifier.
plan_sandbox_pin_hint: Pin a cell, then click another to compare departures, transit times, and ∆v side by side.
panel_gallery_credit: Imagery via Wikimedia Commons and the NASA Images API. Originals © the operating agency (NASA, ESA, JAXA, CSA, Roscosmos, CNSA, ISRO, MBRSC, etc.) and reused under public-domain or Creative Commons (CC BY / CC BY-SA) terms — see each Commons file page for full attribution.
panel_gallery_credit_agency: Imagery © {agency_name}, sourced via Wikimedia Commons and the NASA Images API. Reused under public-domain or Creative Commons (CC BY / CC BY-SA) terms — see each Commons file page for full attribution.
image_credit_modifications: Modified for web display (resized / re-encoded). Originals available at source.
no_endorsement_disclaimer: Use of agency imagery here does not imply endorsement. Trademarks remain property of their respective owners.
library_intro_body_1: Every external link Orrery sends you to lives here. Each entry shows where it goes, in what language, what kind of source it is, and when our link checker last saw it alive.
library_intro_body_2: Like the bill of materials at /credits, this page exists to keep us honest about who gets the click. We send you to operator portals when one exists, native-language Wikipedia when the mission was flown by a non-English-speaking agency, and fall back to English Wikipedia when nothing else covers the entity.
link_credit_verified_title: Date when the link was last reachable from Orrery's link-checker (ADR-051).
credits_intro_body_1: Orrery exists because the world's space agencies, operators, and the Wikimedia community make their work freely available. NASA, ESA, JAXA, ISRO, CNSA, Roscosmos, MBRSC, the Canadian Space Agency, SpaceX, Blue Origin, the Wikimedia Commons contributors, and Wikipedia editors have collectively built a public archive of planetary exploration that is unmatched in human history. Thank you.
credits_intro_body_2: This page is the full bill of materials for everything we redistribute. Every photograph, illustration, texture, and quoted or paraphrased editorial fragment in Orrery has an entry below — original publisher, source link, license, and the surfaces of the app where it appears.
panel_no_links: No external links available for this entry yet.
panel_orbit_shape_meta: ORBIT SHAPE · e={e} · perihelion vs aphelion ×{ratio}
panel_dist_row: {mkm} M km from Sun · rotation {rot} days
panel_source_iau: Source: IAU · JPL Planetary Fact Sheets · J2000 epoch
sizes_source: Diameters in km · Source: IAU · NASA
fly_demo_hint: This is a placeholder free-return demo. Pick a real mission to see its actual launch window.
lib_empty: No missions match the current filters.
lib_load_failed: Failed to load the mission library. Try refreshing the page.
mp_gallery_empty: No photos available for this mission yet.
mp_gallery_credit: Mission imagery via Wikimedia Commons and the NASA Images API. Originals © the operating agency (NASA, ESA, JAXA, ISRO, CNSA, Roscosmos, MBRSC, SpaceX, Blue Origin, etc.) and reused under public-domain or Creative Commons (CC BY / CC BY-SA) terms — see each Commons file page for full attribution.
mp_gallery_credit_agency: Mission imagery © {agency_name}, sourced via Wikimedia Commons and the NASA Images API. Reused under public-domain or Creative Commons (CC BY / CC BY-SA) terms — see each Commons file page for full attribution.
mp_no_links: No external links recorded for this mission.
mp_flight_caveat_sparse: Sparse public records — some values unavailable.
mp_flight_caveat_unknown: Flight data not yet researched for this mission.
fly_load_failed: Failed to load mission. Showing default ORRERY-1 free-return.
fly_flight_caveat_sparse: SPARSE PUBLIC RECORDS — SOME VALUES MISSING
fly_capcom_no_events: No events recorded for this mission.
fly_fd_phase_departure_body: C3 is the squared launch energy a vehicle has after escaping Earth. A positive C3 means the spacecraft is leaving Earth's gravity well; the higher the number, the faster the cruise.
fly_fd_phase_injection_title: Trans-X injection · committing to the arc
fly_fd_phase_injection_body: The injection burn (TLI for the Moon, TMI for Mars) is the largest single ∆v of the trip. After it, the engine is off and the vehicle coasts on a Keplerian arc all the way to the destination.
fly_fd_phase_cruise_body: Coasting along a Hohmann transfer — the most fuel-efficient ellipse that touches both planets' orbits. The Sun is the only force; vis-viva gives the speed at every point. No fuel burning, no engine — coast time is free.
fly_fd_phase_approach_body: Closing on the destination. V∞ is the excess speed at infinity — the speed the spacecraft would have if the planet's gravity weren't there. It sets the minimum braking ∆v before any orbit insertion.
fly_fd_phase_arrival_body: The arrival burn slows the vehicle from its hyperbolic V∞ down below the destination's escape velocity, capturing it into a closed orbit. Miss this burn and the spacecraft flies past forever.
why_caveat_reconstructed_body: Public flight records vary by mission. RECONSTRUCTED means we synthesised numbers from secondary sources (trajectory papers, agency press kits, peer-reviewed reanalysis). They're our best honest estimate, not raw telemetry.
why_caveat_sparse_body: Some agencies released only partial flight data. SPARSE flags missions where headline numbers exist but key parameters (like mid-cruise speeds or per-burn ∆v) were never made public.
why_caveat_unknown_body: We haven't yet researched flight data for this mission. The mission is real and historically documented, but the structured numbers used for visualisations aren't sourced yet.
why_dla_body: DLA is the latitude of the spacecraft's outbound velocity vector relative to Earth's equator. It constrains which launch sites can reach the trajectory: a high-latitude DLA needs a high-inclination launch and costs ∆v.
why_mass_tli_body: The wet mass right after the trans-X injection burn drives every downstream calculation: cruise ∆v budget, structural loads, even the entry mass for landers. Two missions can have similar payloads but very different masses at TLI depending on staging.
why_semi_major_axis_body: Half the long axis of an elliptical orbit — the single most important orbit parameter. Pins the average distance from the Sun and (via Kepler's third law) the orbital period. If you only know one number about an orbit, you want this one.
why_eccentricity_body: How squashed the ellipse is. e=0 is a perfect circle; e→1 is a knife-edge ellipse. High eccentricity means big swings in distance and speed between perihelion (close, fast) and aphelion (far, slow) — the orbit feels lopsided.
why_inclination_body: The orbit's tilt versus the reference plane (ecliptic for heliocentric orbits, equator for Earth orbits). High-inclination orbits cost more ∆v to reach but unlock unique geometries — polar imaging, sun-synchronous lighting, north-south coverage.
why_orbital_period_body: How long one full lap around the Sun (or parent body) takes. Tied to semi-major axis by Kepler's third law: P² ∝ a³. The further out, the slower; Neptune's year is 165 Earth years.
why_perihelion_body: The closest point of the orbit to the Sun (or whatever the orbit is around). The fastest point — vis-viva: small r → large v. For comets, perihelion is when the show happens; for spacecraft, it's the cheapest place to do an Oberth burn.
why_aphelion_body: The farthest point of the orbit from the Sun (or parent body). The slowest point — vis-viva: large r → small v. Good place to nudge the orbit cheaply (small ∆v moves the opposite side a lot) but bad for big burns.
why_axial_tilt_body: The angle between a body's spin axis and the perpendicular to its orbit. Earth's 23.4° gives us seasons. Uranus's 98° means it rolls along its orbit on its side. The tilt also sets where the sun rises and sets at landing sites — critical for solar-power planning.
why_mean_velocity_body: The average orbital speed. Closer-in bodies move fast (Mercury averages 47 km/s); outer bodies crawl (Neptune averages 5.4 km/s). Vis-viva ties velocity to position on the ellipse — perihelion is fast, aphelion is slow.
why_solar_mass_body: Mass sets gravity, gravity sets every orbit. The Sun is 333,000× Earth's mass and dominates the solar system; that one number, with Kepler's laws, predicts where every planet is at every moment.
why_peak_speed_body: The fastest the spacecraft moves in the Sun's frame during cruise — usually right after the trans-X injection burn or near perihelion of the transfer arc. Tells you the kinetic energy budget the vehicle is working with.
why_entry_velocity_body: The speed the vehicle hits the destination's atmosphere at, after gravitational pull has accelerated it. Sets the heat-shield design — every km/s extra means dramatically more heating during entry, descent, and landing.
why_periapsis_body: The orbit's closest point to the destination after insertion. Low periapsis = closer to the planet (better imaging, more drag) but more atmospheric heating; high periapsis = wider orbit, less heating, more fuel needed to get there.
why_arrival_inclination_body: The tilt of the captured orbit versus the destination's equator. Polar orbits (90°) see the whole surface every day but cost more ∆v at insertion. Equatorial orbits (0°) are cheap but see only a band.
why_dv_required_body: The number adds three pieces: the launch energy needed to escape Earth (C3), the cruise burns to shape the transfer ellipse, and the orbit-insertion burn at arrival (zero for a flyby). Cells in the cheap teal lobe sit near a Hohmann minimum; ridges flag synodic gaps where physics makes you wait or pay.
why_earth_altitude_body: Altitude bands (LEO ≤ 2 000 km, MEO ≤ 35 786, GEO at 35 786, HEO above) trade off ground resolution, coverage, communication latency, and launch cost. Operators pick altitude to match the mission — imaging wants low, GPS wants medium, broadcast wants geosynchronous.
why_earth_period_body: Period and altitude are locked together by Kepler's third law. LEO satellites complete a lap every ~90 minutes, MEO every ~12 hours, GEO every 24 hours (matching Earth's rotation). The period decides how often the satellite passes a given ground point.
why_earth_inclination_body: Inclination is the orbit's tilt versus Earth's equator. 0° (equatorial) is cheap from low-latitude launch sites; 51.6° (ISS) is the lowest inclination Russian launches reach without flying over populated areas; ~98° (sun-synchronous) keeps the same lighting for imaging. It's a constraint, not a choice.
why_landing_site_body: Landing sites are a tradeoff: equatorial latitudes are cheap and warm but low-information; polar sites preserve volatiles (water ice in permanently shadowed craters) and are scientifically rich but costly. Specific coordinates target a geological feature — a crater rim, an ancient lakebed, a fresh impact site.
why_surface_time_body: Surface duration is bounded by power (solar charge or RTG), thermal cycling, and crew life-support consumables. Apollo's lunar-day window was about 3 Earth days; Mars rovers ride out years on RTG; static landers usually fail when their batteries drop below freezing.
why_eva_time_body: Extravehicular activity (EVA) is the highest-risk, highest-reward portion of a crewed mission. Suit consumables, micrometeoroid exposure, and CO₂ scrubbing limits cap each EVA at about 8 hours. Total EVA time on a mission is the real measure of how much surface science got done.
why_samples_body: Returned samples can be analysed with mass spectrometers, electron microscopes, isotope dating — instruments that are too big or power-hungry to fly. Apollo brought back 382 kg; Chang'e 5 brought 1.7 kg of younger far-side regolith. Each kilogram unlocks decades of follow-up science.
why_module_mass_body: Mass to LEO is the unforgiving currency of station construction. Every kilogram needs ~9.4 km/s of ∆v to reach orbit; that drives launch-vehicle choice and dictates which modules can fly together. Big modules (Zarya, Tianhe) need heavy-lift; small ones can hitch on cargo flights.
why_module_length_body: Module length is constrained by the rocket's payload fairing — the aerodynamic nose cone that protects the module during launch. Falcon 9's fairing is 13.2 m tall; Long March 5B's is 20 m. Modules longer than the fairing simply can't fly on that rocket.
why_station_altitude_body: Both stations sit in low Earth orbit, ~400 km up. Below ~330 km atmospheric drag eats orbital energy faster than the visiting cargo ships can re-boost. Above ~500 km the inner Van Allen radiation belt starts to bite, and crewed-vehicle range drops. The 350-450 km band is the sweet spot.
why_station_inclination_body: Inclination is set by the launch site, not designer preference. ISS at 51.6° is the lowest inclination Russian Soyuz launches can reach without dropping spent stages on populated land. Tiangong at 41.5° is reachable from Wenchang in southern China without overflying populated areas. Each choice trades launch ∆v for ground-coverage range.
why_station_period_body: Kepler's third law: at ~400 km the period is locked at about 92 minutes. That's 16 sunrises and 16 sunsets per crew day — the body's circadian rhythm gets hammered, which is why crews live on synthetic 24-hour Mission Time set to UTC.
why_station_sunrises_body: 92-minute orbits × 24 hours = 15.6 laps per day, each crossing the day/night terminator twice. Crews see roughly 16 sunrises and 16 sunsets daily. The cabin uses scheduled lighting that follows UTC so circadian rhythm doesn't desynchronise.
microgravity_legend_zenith_nadir_desc: Up (away from Earth) and down (toward Earth). Matters for solar-array pointing + radiator placement.
microgravity_legend_prograde_retrograde_: Direction of orbital motion + its opposite. Reboost burns fire prograde; deboost burns fire retrograde.
microgravity_legend_port_starboard_desc: Left and right of the velocity vector. Truss segments named P1/P3/P4/P5 (port) and S1/S3/S4/S5 (starboard) follow this convention.
science_layer_soi_desc: Translucent rings showing where each body's gravity dominates.
science_layer_hover_desc: Live numbers (speed, distance, gravity) when hovering a planet.
science_layer_gravity_desc: Force arrows from each body, log-scaled so all are visible.
science_layer_velocity_desc: Tangent arrows showing instantaneous heliocentric speed.
science_layer_centripetal_desc: Inward acceleration arrows balancing gravity (F = ma).
science_layer_apsides_desc: Perihelion / aphelion markers + the live ν = 42° angle.
science_layer_coast_desc: Where the spacecraft would drift if the engine cut right now.
science_layer_conics_desc: Names the current arc shape (ellipse / hyperbola / etc.) live.
science_layer_microgravity_desc: Three colour-keyed body axes — zenith / nadir, prograde / retrograde, port / starboard.
science_layer_atmosphere_desc: Translucent shell at the body's effective atmospheric edge — Kármán line on Earth, ~120 km on Mars.
science_layer_tidal_lock_desc: Marks the Moon's near-side (always-Earth-facing) hemisphere — the result of synchronous rotation.
science_layer_ozone_desc: Polar ozone-depletion zones — Antarctic + Arctic — that open seasonally as ClO and BrO catalyse O₃ destruction in the stratosphere.
conics_blurb_circle: Effectively constant radius — speed and distance never change.
conics_blurb_ellipse: Bound orbit. Sun at one focus; speed peaks at perihelion, drops at aphelion.
conics_blurb_parabola: Knife-edge escape — exactly enough speed to leave but never to return.
conics_blurb_hyperbola: Unbound. Excess speed beyond escape; the body keeps going forever.
earth_canvas_label: Earth and its satellites in 3D. Drag to orbit, pinch or scroll to zoom, click any object for details.
moon_canvas_label: Interactive Moon map with landing sites. Click any site marker for details.
moon_load_failed: Failed to load Moon landing sites.
iss_fallback_perf: 3D mode looked slow on this device — switched to the list. Use the toggle to try 3D again.
iss_fallback_memory: Low-memory mode: list view first. Use the toggle to try 3D.
iss_hud_hint: Drag to orbit · tap a module
iss_canvas_aria: Interactive 3D model of the International Space Station. Drag to orbit the view; tap a module to open details.
explore_canvas_aria_3d: 3D solar system. Drag to orbit, scroll or pinch to zoom. Click the Sun and planets for details.
explore_canvas_aria_2d: 2D top-down solar system. Drag to pan, scroll or pinch to zoom. Tap the Sun and planets for details.
explore_layer_tip_planets: Toggle visibility of the eight major planet orbits
explore_layer_tip_dwarfs: Toggle visibility of dwarf planets (Pluto, Eris, Ceres, Haumea, Makemake)
explore_layer_tip_comets: Toggle visibility of comet trajectories (Halley, Hale-Bopp, and others)
explore_layer_tip_interstellar: Interstellar visitors — show or hide objects passing through the Solar System (e.g. ʻOumuamua)
earth_layer_tip_telescopes: Astronomy telescopes — Hubble, JWST, Chandra, XMM, Gaia
earth_layer_tip_nav: Navigation constellations — GPS, Galileo, GLONASS, BeiDou
earth_layer_tip_lunar: Lunar orbiters — LRO, Clementine, Chandrayaan-1, Chang'e 1/2, and others
earth_layer_tip_orbit_rings: Show or hide orbital ring lines (satellites stay visible)
moon_layer_tip_surface: Show or hide landers, rovers, and crashed surface vehicles
moon_layer_tip_orbiters: Show or hide active and historical lunar orbiters
moon_layer_tip_orbit_rings: Show or hide orbital ring lines (spacecraft stay visible)
mars_layer_tip_surface: Show or hide landers, rovers, and crashed surface vehicles
mars_layer_tip_orbiters: Show or hide active and historical Mars orbiters
mars_layer_tip_orbit_rings: Show or hide orbital ring lines (spacecraft stay visible)
mars_layer_tip_traverses: Show or hide rover traverse paths (Curiosity, Perseverance, Opportunity, Spirit)
fly_canvas_aria_3d: 3D mission arc. Drag to orbit, scroll or pinch to zoom.
mars_load_failed: Failed to load Mars sites. Please refresh.
mars_meta_description: Every artefact humanity has placed on Mars — landers, rovers, and active orbiters — on a navigable 3D globe with full mission history.
tiangong_fallback_perf: 3D mode looked slow on this device — switched to the list. Use the toggle to try 3D again.
tiangong_fallback_memory: Low-memory mode: list view first. Use the toggle to try 3D.
tiangong_hud_hint: Drag to orbit · tap a module
tiangong_canvas_aria: Interactive 3D model of the Tiangong space station. Drag to orbit the view; tap a module to open details.
landing_page_title: Orrery · A solar system explorer for the browser
landing_meta_description: Browser-based solar system explorer, mission simulator, and encyclopedia. Real orbital mechanics. 36 missions. 14 languages. Open source, no accounts, no tracking.
landing_hero_tagline: A solar system explorer for the browser.
landing_section_what_p1: Orrery is a browser-based solar system explorer, mission simulator, and encyclopedia. Real orbital mechanics under the hood — Keplerian two-body propagation, Lambert solvers for transfer trajectories, the same equations real flight teams use. No backend. No accounts. Runs offline once it's loaded.
landing_section_what_p2: It's not a flight planner. There's no API to NORAD, no real-time TLE updates, no live launch-window predictions. The numbers are physically correct but stylised; the missions in the catalogue are real but their flight arcs are teaching models, not propagated state vectors.
landing_section_what_p3: Designed for everyone — curious lay people, students, space enthusiasts, mission planners who want a sandbox. Built for the browser so it works on any device with a screen. Open source under MIT. The catalogue covers 36 real missions across the inner solar system plus the Moon and four outer-planet destinations.
landing_section_why_p1: The project started during the lead-up to Artemis II. NASA was about to send four humans around the Moon for the first time since 1972, and the public conversation around it was almost entirely uninformed. People didn't know what 'free-return trajectory' meant. They didn't know why Mars launch windows happen every 26 months. They didn't know the rocket equation.
landing_section_why_p2: Orrery is the answer to that. Spaceflight is built on physics that's been understood since Newton. Most of what looks magical is actually visible — you can see the orbits, you can compute the transfer arcs, you can read every formula. The simulator is the front door; the encyclopedia is the textbook; together they're the project.
landing_section_tour_p1: Start at /explore. Spin the solar system. Click any planet to read its orbital elements — semi-major axis, eccentricity, true anomaly. The numbers are live.
landing_section_tour_p2: Find a mission you care about in /missions — Curiosity, Apollo 11, anything from Sputnik onward. Read its FLIGHT tab to see the actual ∆v budget broken down across the five phases of every spaceflight: launch, trans-X injection, cruise, orbit insertion, EDL.
landing_section_tour_p3: When something doesn't make sense — when a number on the HUD or a label on a chart confuses you — every term is linked to /science. The encyclopedia is meant to be read in order or hopped through depending on what you need.
landing_section_tour_p4: /plan and /fly are the deeper end. /plan computes porkchop diagrams for any destination — pick a launch year, see when the windows open. /fly takes a mission and walks you through its trajectory phase by phase. The HUD shows everything in real units.
landing_card_explore_desc: Spin, zoom, click any planet for live orbital elements.
landing_card_plan_desc: Porkchop diagrams for any destination — see when nature makes the trip cheap.
landing_card_fly_desc: Walk a mission's arc phase by phase, with ∆v and timing live in the HUD.
landing_card_missions_desc: Real spacecraft from Sputnik to Artemis II — flight data for every one.
landing_card_earth_desc: Active satellites, GPS, ISS, JWST — everything in our backyard.
landing_card_moon_desc: Every Apollo landing site, every Soviet rover, every active orbital asset.
landing_card_mars_desc: 27 missions plotted on a real DEM — Curiosity's path, Perseverance's cache, the lot.
landing_card_iss_desc: 18 pressurised modules, every visiting vehicle, full station geometry.
landing_card_tiangong_desc: China's station — every module, every visiting Shenzhou and Tianzhou.
landing_card_science_desc: 54 sections across 8 tabs. Every formula, every named law, hand-coded diagrams.
landing_card_fleet_desc: Historical and current spacecraft with crews, payloads, and outcomes.
landing_footer_about: Orrery is open-source under the MIT license. No accounts. No tracking. One functional cookie stores your explicit locale choice (per ADR-057).
